CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We notice that English starch is being very widely
advertised in the Irish Press at present. That fact
should stiffen the backs of Irish Irelanders in favour of
Irish-made starch. Evidently the English starch-
maker thinks the Irish market is worth fighting for.
And so it is ; and we hope that our readers will do their
part in not only retaining the Irish manufacturers’
hold on the starch markst, but in greatly increasing it.
Our readers do a lot for Irish manufacture. One con-
sumer by merely insisting on being supplied with cer-
tain articles of ITrish manufacture in a shop not only
does an individual’s part, but frequently is the imme-
djate cause of a business house opening an account with
an Irish manufacturer or this wholesale agent for a
* particular line of goods in which there was no previous
dealing. We know of many instances where accounts

have been opened merely by the insistence of one

customer.

Irish-made starch is a commodity concerning which
the demand is not in excess of the supply, and our
readers could help to develop the industry by insisting
on being supplied with Irish-made starch by the shop-
keepers with whom they deal.

We are sorry to hear that many convents and other
institutions are not supporters of Irish starch. In the
course of a report of the proceedings at one of the meet-
ings of the Cork Development Association, last year, we
read that ‘‘ The attention of the Council was drawn to
the fact that the majority of the public institutions
throughout Ireland have not yet commenced to use
Irish-made starch, of which article £47,340 worth was
imported into Ireland in 1904. There are three Irish
firms manufacturing starch, one of which is the Silver-
spring Starch Co., of Glanmire, Co. Cork, and they
supply as good quality as any imported. The Council
considered it a sad reflection on the institutions using
imported starch that they, supported as they are by the
money of the Irish people, should prevent numbers of
Irishmen and women from procuring employment at
liome owing to their unfairness in refusing to use Irish-
made starch.  Council stated that it would be very
reluctant to publish the names of these institutions, but
unless they speedily change their policy of procuring
their funds in Ireland and procuring their supplies else-
where, it will consider it its duty to mention their
names.’’

We do not think that we would suffer from any
feeling of reluctance about publishing them if we got
the names of institutions that refused to support Irish
starch on its merits. Naturally one does not wish to
pick out one or two institutions and pillory them if
large numbers of other institutions are in like case.
The report from the proceedings of the Cork Industrial
Development Association refers to three Irish starch-
makers, but we only know of two. One of them, the
Silverspring starch, of Glanmire, Cork, is not hiding its
head when the British maker is flaunting his in the
columns of so many Irish papers seeking what Irish
custom he may devour.

We hear that the Great Northern Railway people are
dismissing a number of hands. What is the cause of
this? Was the Railway previously over-staffed, or are
the authorities, in view of possible State purchase,
economising with a view to showing bigger profits?

Last week we drew attention to the intolerant con-
duct of the Earl of Carysfort in refusing to let the Marl-
borough Hall, in Arklow, to the Ivish National Fores-
ters’ Dramatic Club, who wished to stage a play, the
proceeds of which were to go to relieve the distress of
those who suffered as a consequence of a recent disaster
to some Arklow sailors. Another case of miserable in-
tolerance is reported from Clogher, in the Co. Tyrone,
and in this case also the Foresters are concerned. The
Courthouse at Clogher was refused to those who wished
to hold an entertainment in aid of the funds of the
band of the local branch of the Irish National Fores-
ters! The Courthouse at Clogher is the only suitable
hall for a popular entertainment. As the law stands,
though the .Courthouse is maintained out of the local
rates, yet the custody and control of it is vested in the
High Sheriff. So though the people must pay to main-
tain the Courthouse, an Orange or bigoted unit of the -

- “‘carrion crow”’

r " party who may happen to be High Sheriff
can indulge his intolerance. The High Sheriff in this
case is one Mr. Carmichael Ferrall.  This intoletant

~ man is a locally well-known Orange and Unionist

ranter. A few weeks previous to his intolerant exer-
cise of petty power this man granted the use of the
Cou_rtl!ouse, which the people’s money maintains, to the
Unionist Party for some religious lecture; but when
the Foresters’ Society want the use of it Mr. Carmichael
Ferrall, of Augher Castle, turns Orange with pious in-
dignation, and refuses! No High Sheriff had ever pre-
viously refused the Clogher Courthouse to any section of
the people for a legitimate purpose, and until this par-
ticular ornament of Orange platforms came on the scene
it was only necessary to apply to the Sub-Sheriff.

Mr. Ferrall, of “‘the carrion crow’’ party, first refused
the Courthouse on the ground that the object of the
meeting was political. He was promptly told that the
Foresters are a nonm-political body. As he could not
refuse it on political grounds, and had stated that he
did not refuse it on religious grounds, he was asked on
what grounds he refused it.

The Eloquent Mr. Birrell was questioned on the
matter in Parliament. He referred the question to the
intolerant High Sheriff himself, who told Mr. Birrell
that he refused the use of the Courthouse on the ground
that the concert was to be held for the promotion of
party politics. Mr. Birrell admitted that the Court-
house was maintained out of the rates, but he stated
that the Government have no power to interfere with
the High Sheriff’s discretion as to the purposes for which
he may permit a Courthouse to be used when not re-
quired for the administration of the law. Poor
“ Liberal ’ Government! What between ‘‘the Trea-
sury *> and this intolerant High Sheriff of the ‘ carrion
crow’’ party it is to be pitied! If it doesn’t take care its
carcase may be eaten by Messrs. Craig, Moore and Co.

Our information about this piece of impudent intoler-
ance is taken from various items concerning it in our
contemporary, the ‘‘ Ulster Herald,”’ and with our con-
temporary we hope that ‘‘ The Nationalists of Clogher
should follow this matter up, and if they only succeed
in changing the anomalous law that gives officials control
of public property they will have done a good deal to
end once and for all the reign of the remnant of the
Ascendancy oligarchy that exists in Tyrone.”’

Police-Sergeant Byrne is ‘‘severing his connection
with the town of Castleblayney.”” We learn this fact
from a circular signed by ‘“B. Farnan, Secretary.’”’
Policeman Byrne is retiring on pension, and the inevit-
able ““ hat’’ is being sent round Castleblayney. The
preliminary meeting was held in Smyth’s public-house,
in Main Street, and Dr. Clarke was in the chair, and
it was unanimously decided to make a present to the
policeman. About 200 copies of a circular was sent
out calling a further meeting in the Urban Council’s
Office, at which only thirteen attended (we trust Police-
man Byrne is not superstitious), and the majority of
-these thirteen were composed of promoters of this
attempted testimonial, and seven of them were publi-
cans. A publican was moved to the chair on this
momentous occasion, and he made a speech on the
policeman about; to retire on pension. ~ The Chairman
‘wound up with what, we have no doubt, was an oration
that would have done credit to the Eloquent Dempsey
himself by remarking that it would be cold and harsh
to let the sergeant leave them without some little com-

pensation, and so forth. ~Two or three collectors for
each street in the town were appointed to prey on the
inoffending people of Castleblayney. The idea of a
house-to-house collection for this nu.tion-m_a:kmg project
did not meet with unanimous approval, for Mr. Farnan
remarked that it would only be necessary to call on the
dealers, particularly the spirit dealers, and the Chqlr-
man dotted the “i’'s’’ in the * spirit dealer ** suggestion
by remarking that they, the spirit dealers, never got
much trouble from Sergt. Byrne. In response to the 200
circulars, six persons handed in subscriptions, and.th_e
amount added to the subscriptions taken at the prelimi-
nary meeting at Smyth’s public-house made up a total of
£11 5s. We wonder how much did Castleblayney sub-
scribe for the Language Fund? We suppose when the
collection is completed the presentation will be made in
one of the public-houses, and that Sergeant Byrne won t
have very much change when he pays the bill for the
free drinks for the donors, particularly those who sub-
scribe 1s., and go to drink a half a crown’s worth!

The Loughrea Football and Hurling Club is not only
national, but it is actually ‘‘ grand national.”  We
cather the latter fact from a document before us which
is a ticket in connection with what is styled ‘‘ Grand
National Sweep.”” In what part of the County of Gal-
way, or in which other county in Ireland, is the ‘ Grand
National ”’ run, we wonder? Perhaps the Hon. Sec. of
the Loughrea Football and Hurling Club would give us
the information.

There is a vacancy in Killimore, Co. Galway, for the
position of Postmaster. There is, of course, a great
struggle going on for this village appointment, and
several civilian applicants with suitable houses are in
the field ; but there are also a dozen or so policemen—
whether in active ‘“work ’’ or on pension after their
““labour ”’ we cannot say—looking for the job. We
will be interested to hear the results of this civilian
versus ‘“ bobby ’’ contest.

The Keating Branch of the Gaelic League, whose per-
formance of Father Dinneen’s ‘‘Creideamh agus Gorta’
last year in Dublin was a memorable dramatic event,
scored yet another dramatic triumph on Monday night
last, when they gave a representation to a full house at
the Rotunda of Piaras Beaslai’s new play, ‘‘ Cormac na
Coille.””
on the whole, very creditable. The two female char-
acters in the play were most admirably represented by
Cait ni Dhounchadha and by Sile ni Ailgheasa.
‘“ Cormac Riabhach,’’ the hero of the play, was repre-
sented by the author himself, and it is safe to say that
Piaras Beaslai acted the part with great ability.
Thomas O’Muirgheasa also did full justice to the char-
acter of Brian O’Duinnin, ollamh to the Earl of Clan-
carty. Indeed, all the characters acquitted them-
selves so well that it would be invidious.to single out
names for special praise. The play itself is in some
respects a striking piece of work. The language is rich,
flowery and rhetorical. It is at times eloquent. The
historical colouring is well maintained, and the whole
piece is lighted up by the flame of battle and softened
by the assertion of the tenderest affections. It is
Piaras’s first dramatic effort, and it does him credit. A
play of this kind would be listened to with intense in-
terest and breathless attention in the Ivish districts, and
is of itself a justification of Mr. Edward Martyn’s sug-
gostion as to an itinerant dramatic company. The
scone of the play is in and near Blarney Castle, and the
timo is the year 1600, on the occasion of Hugh O’Neill’s

Mr. Field, M.P., has sent us a copy of the answer 
which ho received from the autocrat of the Post Office, 
Mr. Buxton, in reply to his question-" Whether, in 
view of the fact tha.t the local post oftlce store branches 
iri Dublin, Belfast, and Cork have been abolished, and 
a new central department created in Dublin to take 
their place, and that the Irish clerks iu the former 
-branches were informed they could not obtain appoint-
ments under the new department, he will state how 
they have been provided for; and whether a portion of 
the staff has been imported 1 " 
The answer of the autocrat of the Post Office is as 
follows: -The formation of the new Central Stores 
Branch in Duplin is still under consideration, and in the 
circumstances I am not yet in a position to state what 
the composition of the staff will be. 
As I said on the 
18t-h April, in reply to the hon. member for North 
Kilkenny, full regard will be had to the claims of the 
existing staff, and there is no question of dispensing 
with the services of any of them, or of reducing their 
pay. 
That is a fine specimen of an official reply. It will 
b•? noticed that the question '' whether a portion of the 
st,aff has been imported " is completely ignored in the 
''answer" of Mr. Buxton. 
The formation of the 
Central Stores Branch " is still under consideration." 
Well, while it is under consideration is just the period 
to keep Mr. Buxton and his understrappers under 
observation, and we hope that Mr. Field and the mem-
bers of the Postal Service in +):eland will keep their 
attention fixed on this matter.

such a. company if formed on proper lines with ll"ish

l!'ath~r ;r. Brown, P.P., of Glenflesk, Co. Kerry, has 
had a rather sad experience of the law. 
His pre-
decessor in the parish, tho late Father O'Flaherty, left 
£160 for t-he repairs of the Barraduff church and for 
Masses. for his soul to his successor in the parish. 
Of 
course, he did not know who his successor would be, but 
two deposit receipts were drawn up and the money 
lodged to the credit of the Parish Priest of Glenflesk 
and Barraduff without any name being mentioned. 
After the late P .P. 's death there· was law over the £160, 
and the present P .P., Father Brown, naturally defended . 
his claim. As he says : - " I considered it was my strict 
duty to claim this money for the purposes for which it 
was left." 
In the King's Bench the three judges 
unanimously decided in Father Brown's favour, both 
parties getting their costs out of the estate. 
There was 
an appeal, and the judges of Appeal decided that the 
money should not go to Father Brown, and that Father 
Brown was to pay his own costs and the costs of the 
appeal. 
Father Brown, in the course of a letter to us, 
says :-" When I came here I found the Presbytery 
and churches in a truly dilapidated state. 
The church 
at Barraduff is in such a sadly neglected state that the 
Bishop of the diocese described it at the last Synod as 
a ' disgrace to the diocese.' In it there is no altar but 
an old box, no floor but one of mud, with many large 
holes; windows, rotten and decayed; walls, mouldy and 
dirty ; no ' Stations of the Cross,' no ' statues,' none 
even of the Mother of God, before which the poor people 
may make through her their wants and their sorrows 
known to God; no 'proper vestments,' no 'altar furni 0 • 
ture,' not even a decent candlestick, and, worse still, 
the church is too small for the congregation. More than 
300 people have to hear Mass in winter and summer, in 
rain and storm, outside the door, where they have not 
even a chance to hear the word of God preached to 
them." 
Under these distressing circumstances it is indeed 
deplorable that not only should F_ather Brown have lost 
his case on appeal, but that he should have been mulcted 
in heavy costs. 
However, he says:-" The people of 
the parish and myself have lost no time in setting about 
rebuilding this ruined sanctuary of God amongst us. 
They have contributed £210 and carted all the stone 
and sand required, but a considerable sum is still re-
quired to carry (?Ut the extension and repairs of ~he 
church. 
We have lost the £100 left by the late parish 
priest for this purpose, and the judges of Appeal have 
placed on us the additional burden of heavy costs, but 
we have not lost confidence that God. will inspire others 
to help us. 
I appeal to all ' who love justice and hate 
iniquity ' to help us to pay off these costs. 
I appeal to 
evoryone who loves the beauty of God's House and who 
wishes that all should have a decent church wherein 
.to worship God aud hear His Divine Word, to help us 
l;o rebuild the l011g-forgotten and sadly-neglected church 
of Barraduff."

gheda is still burning; in fact, the question exploded" in 
the rooms of the local branch of the U uited Irish League 
recently. It may be remembered that there were about 
sixty applicants for the position, and Mr. Brown, an 
Irishman, and gas manager at Tuam, was eventually 
allowed to compete amongst the selected few. 
Alder-
man Daly proposed Mr. Brown. "His testimonials 
and qualifications he (Alderman Daly) considered as 
good a.s those of any of the others, and then he is an·

And in face of the fact that Mr. Brown, the Irishman,
had been elected by 11 votes to 10 by a Committee of the
Whole House, Mr. Saville was olected subsequently by
11 votes to 10 by the wholo house not in Committee !
We suggested at the time that the Mayor of Drogheda
should in future call himself Mr. Saville-Skeffington.

All that happened in March. A few days ago there
was a meeting of the Drogheda branch of the U.I.L.
Mr. S_avlllc»Skcﬂingtou, if we may so call him and make
an aristocrat of him by the simple device of a hyphen,
presided. Mr. McArdle moved : —“ That we, the mem-
bers of the Drogheda branch of the U.I.L., condemn
the action of the Drogheda Corporation in selecting an
Englishman for the position of a gas manager, and not
giving an Irishman a chance. That we look upon their
action as a grave betrayal of National principles likely
to weaken the confidence of the people in the Local
Government Act, and likely to enervate, if not utterly
destroy, the political or National spirit in the people in
fighting for the freedom of Ireland.”’

Mr. Skeffington, the Mayor, in the course of his
defence, said:—‘‘ T think it is a mistake just now to be
raising those racial distinctions.”” The resolution con-
demning the Drogheda Corporation for its action was
eventually carried, the Mayor dissenting. Perhaps, at

PROTESTANTS AND THE GAELIC LEAGUE.

S. ve h-1peabomn, Secretary of the meeting referred
to below, sends us the following :—‘‘ A meeting of Pro-
testant Gaelic Leaguers was held in the room of the
Apo - Cpaob, 24 Upper O’Connell St., on Saturday;
May 4th, at 8.30 p.m. The object of the meeting was
to consider means of bringing the principles of the
Gaelic League prominently under the notice of Protes-
tants, and inducing them to take their place in the
Gaelic movement. There was a very good attendance,
the chair being taken by Séamap Oéacam. It was
decided to send a deputation to the Dean of St. Pat-
rick’s to ask his consent to having the use of St. Pat-
rick’s Cathedral for a service in Irish once a month,
and also to send a deputation to Professor Murphy,
T.C.D., to obtain his support. The question of holding
lectures in the different parochial halls was also dis-
cussed, and it was decided to communicate with all the
Protestant schools in the city with a view to obtaining
information regarding the status of the Irish language
and history in these schools. The next meeting will
take place on Saturday, 18th May, and due notice con-
cerning it will be given.

ROME.

O a visitor, Rome is a city of never-ending interest.
It can be studied either as Pagan Rome, Rome
of the Popes, or Rome as an Art treasure-house. Pagan
Rome goes back more than seven hundred years before
the Christian era; Rome of .the Popes commences on
the eventful day when St. Peter first entered the gates
of the proud city, which was then the mistress of the
world. The Art-treasures of Rome have been collected
and preserved by various Popes; they include matchless
pictures, unequalled sculptures, and priceless manu-
seripts. Rome attracts, and will always attract, the
historian, the Christian, and the artist. But when a
Catholic has wandered among the countless ruins of the
Roman Forum, and gazed with awe on the stupendous
mass of the Colosseum ; when he has visited St. Peter’s,
the most magnificent temple ever raised by man in
honour of the Creator; when he has seen the treasures
of the Vatican Museum and the Vatican Library,
there is still an unsatisfied wish—he cannot think of
leaving the Eternal City without seeing the spiritual
ruler of -the Catholic world—the Pope. To no person
does this wish appeal more strongly than to an Irish
Catholic; for more than fourteen troubled centuries
have Ireland and Rome been linked in bonds of affec-
tion ; and never once, during that long period, have the
Ivish people ceased to venerate and to obey the suc-
cessors of St. Peter. O’Connell gave a striking proof
of this personal Irish love for Rome; and no Irish
vigitor will leave Rome without visiting the Chapel of
the Trish College to see the spot where the great heart
of the Liberator is enshrined. Nor will such a visitor
fail to visit the Janiculum, so that he may see there 4
the church in which the remains of the great Hugh %
O’Neill and his son still rest.  But when all these
places have been reverentially visited, the visitor still
finds himself saying—‘ I should like to see the Pope.”
There are different ways of seeing the Holy Father.
Let us suppose that the visitor has been so fortunate
ag to obtain an order from the proper authority—and
on the way to obtain such an order something will be
said later on; he sees on the order the day and hour
on which the interview will be granted. Ladies, it may
be observed, are requived to wear a veil, gentlemen go
in evening dress. K e
As the visitor drives towards the great brass door of
the Vatican, St. Peter’s is close on his left; and no
matter how often he may have stood in the noble
Piazza, and gazed on Michael Angelo’s marvellous dome,

THE LEADER.

whole magnificent building. = But having arrived at
the Vatican gate, his attention is soon occupied by other
matters. His “ Order ” is carefully examined, and he
is then permitted to enter. He finds himself in a new
world. The City of Rome is in the hands of the Italian
Government, but the Vatican and all enclosed within
its high walls still remain to the Pope.

On entering the Vatican two things atitract atten-
tion—the magnificent marble steps by which the visitor
gradually ascends, and the gorgeous dress of the Papal
Guards, who are stationed on each landing and at each
door. At length the waiting-room is reached ; here the
visitor takes off the overcoat with which he had covered
his evening dress, and gives it to the cbliging attendants.
He then passes into the reception-room—a fine, large
room, having seats all round. The visitors take their
places so as to form a single line round the four sides
of the room. For a few minutes there is some quiet
conversation, then all is hushed, and his Holiness enters.
He passes along the line of visitors, giving his hand to
each so that the papal ring may be kissed. Sometimes
a visitor speaks to the Holy Father, who briefly replies.
When his Holiness came’to the little group with which
the writer was connected, the Very Rev. Father
O’Keefe spoke in Italian, and said that we were from
Ireland; and the Holy Father, speaking in French—
probably that we might the better understand—said :
““T give them a special benediction.”” In what seemed
a moment, we had kissed the papal ring, and his Holi-
ness had passed on.

The Holy Father looked strong and vigorous; showed
no trace of illness, and seemed to carry his three score
and twelve years with ease. His photographs give a
very correct representation of his general appearance.
When the Pope—accompanied by his attendants, of
whom the Marquis Molony was one—had gone round
the room he stood at the end, and, speaking a few words
to the visitors, added that he would now bless such
beads, crosses, etc., as had been brought; and having
done so he passed to another room. The visitors
gradually made their way to the waiting-room, and,
having again wrapt themselves up, went down the
marble staircase, and passed the gorgeous guards, to
the great brazen door.

A still greater privilege for visitors is to attend a
a Mass said by the Pope. The chapel used holds about
180 or 200, and on the occasion now referred to, it was
quite full. His Holiness said one Mass and then heard
a second. Although there were some two hundred com-
municants, his Holiness gave Holy Communion to each
with his own hand.

On the evening of the feast-day of his Holiness (Feast
of St. Joseph), an enclosed garden in the Vatican was
beautifully lighted up, and the coloured lights were re-
flected by two fountains; two bands played selected
pieces of music; and those who were so fortunate as to
have obtained orders of admission, moved about, or
chatted in groups. After some time his Holiness came
t> an upper window, and, having looked at the illumi-
nations, gave his blessing and went away.

Such are the usual modes of ‘‘seeing the Pope.”
‘When a pilgrimage is made from any of the numerous
countries over which the Holy Father reigns, the privi-
lege of an interview is generally obtained.  Again,
persons of high rank who have important business arc
sometimes admitted to private interviews ; but with such
things an ordinary visitor has nothing to do.

To obtain an interview a visitor should have letters of
introduction, or should be acquainted with some person
of importance in Rome. All matters connected with
interviews are loft to the Major-domo, Monsignor Bis-
leti; and his secretaries attend in an office at the
Vatican to receive applications. The secretary roads
the letters handed in, takes down the applicant’s
address, and asks a few questions. Should the appli-
cation be granted, a special messenger from the Vatican
brings to the visitor, at his hotel, a duly sealed letter,
stating the day and the hour of the interview; and,
as an acknowledgment that this letter has been duly
received, the visitor gives the messenger his card.

The difficulty and the expense of a visit to Rome are

May 11, 1907.

now much less than formerly, and when a large party
travels together the cost is much {educed. If a Pil-
grimage to Rome were organised, it would give those
who might hesitate to travel alone an opp(t')rhumty of
carrying out a wish dear to many hearts—‘‘to see the
Pope.” Now that his Ioliness is pressed round by
bitter and unscrupulous enemies it would be well to
show that he also has subjects whose fidelity has never
wavered since Rome sent St. Patrick to our ancestors.
M. SULLIVAN.

A ROYAL UNIVERSITY CONTRAST.

Y HE note in the LraDER in reference to the installa-

tion of Sir James Creed Meredith as Deputy
Grand King of the Grand Royal Arch Chapter of Ire-
land on the 1st of May suggests many thoughts. Dr.
Meredith has a very comfortable berth in the Royal
University of Treland, of which, in the eyes of the
undergraduates, he is the autocrat. Yet when King
Ldward visited Ireland, and his ‘“loyal * subjects were
tripping over each other in their anxiety for titles, or
mayhap to show their loyalty, the address from the
secret, society called the I'reemasons was presented by
Dr. Meredith, and that from the Royal University by
his junior colleague, Dr. Magrath. This I would inter-
pret to signify that the interests of masonry rank higher
in the opinion of Dr. Meredith than those of the Uni-
versity. Otherwise the point is unimportant. Addresses,
as a rule, are valueless, and in Ireland are mainly re-
served for policemen and bank clerks. A person un-
acquainted with the ways of the Ascendancy party in
Ireland would expect that one of the chief Executive
officers of a ‘‘ half-and-half >’ University would not un-
necessarily assert his political or religious opinions. Yet,
on the other hand, one must admire a man who fear-
lessly proclaims his allegiance to even masonry—it
shows a little grit, at all events. Of course, in this
strange land of ours, to be opposed to the people is to
be respected; to be with the people in all their
cherished aspirations is to be popular, perhaps, but to
be despised. Tence a man is usually safe in flaunting
public opinion, for he ensures to himself the influence
of those who both know how to, and will, use influence ;
while he brings on himself the opposition of those only
who, like Brutus when ‘‘much enforced,’”” show ‘a
hasty spark and straight’’ are ‘“cold again.”  Sit
tight and say nothing—then you have little to fear
from national opposition of any sort; the opposition
cools off very quickly, and then you may, with impu-
nity, say what you like.

Now there are two Secretaries of the Royal Univer-
sity. They are both aliens in the University.  Dr.
Meredith is a graduate of the ‘ Spelling Bee * Univer-
sity, admitted to an ad eundem degree in the Royal.
Dr. Magrath, his Papist junior colleague, is a graduate
of London University with an %onoris causa degree of
LL.D. in the Royal in virtue of his position.  Dr.
Meredith, on account of his position in the masonic
world, had a kuighthood conferred on him, T presume
to mark King Edward’s love of Irish Idolators!

Now Sir James Meredith (or is it Deputy Grand King
Meredith?) is a fighting Protestant (I use that loose,
general, and to me almost meaningless, term by which
non-Catholic Christians call themselves), a prominent
magon, and a convinced Unionist, and openly so, while
Dr. Magrath is a Catholic of the tamest class. He
figures at moetings of the Society for the Prevention of
Cruelty to Children, but would be loath to advocate the
Prevention of Cruelty to Papists. Why is a Catholic
holding a prominent position, where he might be useful,
always the veriest of tames? In the case of a
Catholic advanced to an important position in a
Government office where the atmosphere is non-Catho-
lic and masonic, I can seo a reason why that Catholio
may aim at self-suppression. T neither respect him nor
do T justify him for it—but there is something intelli-
gible in it all. But in the case of a man occupying a
permanent position equal in all things, save in seniority
of appointment, to his non-Catholic colleague—as 1s

11, 1907,

th the Secretaries of the Royal University—
o see why the non-Catholic should be rampant
he Catholic apologetically tame. Wera the case
ine, I should regard the action of my colleague as a
- challenge to me to do likewise— but only with a dif-
ference.’”” T would much prefer to be a physical rather
than a moral coward. And moral cowardice—the foar
of being considered aggressive; the fear of inviting
even unjustifiable sarcasm—is, I am afraid, our weak
* point all along the line.  Catholicism is something to

be proud of. We know it; no truth is more evident
to us. Vet we do not act as if it were a living truth
within us. We seem to scek at times the unnatural
divorcement of our religion and our everyday life. Yet
we must be Catholics all the time or not at all.

As things stand, Meredith’s opinions will, in
the popular mind, come to be identified with the Royal
University—Magrath being silent. And this notwith-
standing the large percentage of Catholic and Nationa-
list graduates and undergraduates belonging to the
University. Meredith is Deputy Grand King among
the masons; is Dr. Joseph Magrath even an ordinary
member of the Catholic Defence Society? If not, why
not? LOHENGRIN.

and some amusement the articles on Irish spelling

from entering the arena in the hope that my friend

“Mapdan ” or some other successful student of Irish

~ would come to the aid of Mr. P. J. O’Sullivan and ‘“ A

f Gaelic Leaguer,”” and attempt to show that the spelling

of Irish is not the cliff that bars the average student’s

way to acquiring in a couple of years a good working

knowledge of modern Irish—spoken and written. T

am not a linguist, neither am I an authority on phone-

tics, but I can, I think, without any approach to egotism

or self-adulation, lay claim to having been a fairly suc-

cessful student of Irish, although I had nothing to help

me in my study of the language except the much-decried

O’Growney ‘‘ Simple Lessons,”’ a few Irish text-books,

and the help of a few native Irish speakers that I made
out in Waterford city and county.

‘“ Chanel ”’ began this discussion with an article in
the LEAper entitled, I think, ‘“ The Confession of an
‘O’Growneyite ’’ (unsuccessful). With your permission,
Mzr. Editor, I shall try and give briefly the confessions
‘of another O’Growneyite (fairly successful), as the
shortest and clearest way of giving what I might call
the other side of the story. Here it is:—Some years
- ago I came across Dr. O’Hickey’s lecture, ‘“ The True
~ National Idea,” published as a propagandist pamphlet

by the Gaelic League. Read it three times in one

night.  Became convinced that the preservation and
spread of the Irish language was a national necessity,
and that I, an Irishman, was in duty bound to take
advantage of the opportunity offered by Gaelic League
branches, simply cheap text-books, etc., for learning it.

quently no chance of attending classes at branch of

ocket, and studied whenever opportunity offered.
‘After 0'Growney books read Tavs Taba and Taovs

During all these years I never heard one of the dozen

language presented any difficulty to them. Two of
them are now Irish teachers like myself, and the rest
have the pleasure of having done their duty to Ireland
by learning the language that should have been theirs
from childhood. What has surprised me in connection
with this spelling controversy is that those who allege
that the spelling of Irish bars the way of the student
are men of the highest literary attainments, some of
them University graduates. Reading their complaints
of failure I could not help thinking of an experience
I had last year at the Co. Wexford Feis at Enniscorthy.

I was assisting at the inter-branch language competi-
tions.  Those competing were farm labourers, smiths,
carpenters and farmers’ sons and daughters. I was
astounded at the accuracy and fluency with which they

studies, but the reasons that I assign for their failure

are (1) mental laziness, which prevents many who really

desire to know Irish, and fully understand its national
importance and significance, from a national point of

view, from making the necessary exertion required to =
learn it; (2) the absence of what I might call ‘the
spirit of the Gaelic League’’ in many students who
join Gaelic League branches; (3) failure of students
through timidity and fear of ridicule to utilise the native
Trish speakers in their vicinity. These, and not so-
called superfluous: Irish consonants, are, in my opinion,
some of the ‘‘cliffs that bar the way’’ of students of
Trish. In a future article I hope to deal with some
of the cliffs that bar the work of the League as an
organisation.

has been going on wrong lines. IHere is a little advice

“ CORMAC” REPLIES TO “INQUIRER.”

MI NQUIRER'S "’ last article is characterized by

such an utter distortion of fact and puerile per-
version of logic that I have some difficulty in discover-
ing my own identity anywhere within the length and
breadth of it. Not merely has he persisted in mis-
representing my expressed views and putting such
words as ‘“anti-clerical,”” “anti-Catholic,” and ‘‘cor-
ruption ’—terms which I never used—into my mouth,
but he has in addition so mixed me up with “ Eirean-
nac,” that the main issues, whidh lie altogether outside
his precious questions, have been quite confused, and,
mayhap, deliberately so, in his handling of them.
And the climax is reached when he assumes towards the.
close of his pavagraph, marked 4, that I, too, am a
priest. Now I never set myself to auswer In-
quirer’s "’ original queries. T stated distinctly that
I only proposed to make some observations arising out
of vather than on them; and these observations, I may
say, I intended for the eye and the ear of your readers
in general rather than for the information or enlighiten-
ment of “Inquirer.”” I disoussed and have been dis-
cussing this managerial question from the point of
view of the practical man of the world rather than
from that of the educational expert, the canonist and
the theologian, in which triple capacity Inquirer’”
poses.

Now to come

back to the rcal reasons that dictate

DON. Central and comfortable.  Special terms f

the attitude of the Church in its relations with Primary
Education in Ireland. There are really, as every good
Catholic knows, no abstruse canonical or theological
subtleties at all in question. Every system of educa-
bion that has hitherto been launched on this country,
commencing with the  Parish” and ending with the
& Nat.,lgna/l ** schools, was devised to Protestantise and
Anglicise the people.  This fact of itself would be
sufficient justification of the claim for clerical control,
even if there was no such factor as the inherent rights
of the Church to be reckoned with.

. But. “Inquirer ”’ tells us that the danger of prosely-

tism is now dead. Possibly. But worse threatens.
The tendency to-day in nearly every one of the great
States most “advanced in civilization is towards. Seou-
larism in the schools; and we have been told on the
highest political authority that it is the only alterna-
tive to the rejected. Bill of the present Government.
When our neighbour’s house is on fire is not tho time
to abate the watch on our own, and for a further im-
portant reason too. Our very latest created Depart-
ment charged with educational functions—the Agri-
cultural and Technical—is a purely secular one. And
yet, strangely and suggestively enough, it has, as
stated in my first article, been invested with a control-
ling power in the co-ordination of the various systems
below the University. Tt has three representatives on
the Consultative Committee against one each from the
Intermediate and National Boards respectively. And
when it is borne in mind that the National Board
representative is the redoubtable Dr. Starkie himself,
and that the Intermediate man, however distinguished,
is not, I believe, exaotly the person whom the clerical
managers would choose for a champion, the Church
authorities cannot be blamed if, in the interest of
faith and morals, they keep as firm a grip as they can
on the Primary system for the present. Moreover,
even Dr. Starkie complained some time ago that
Plunkett’s purely secular department with its majority
on the Co-ordination Committee exercised an alto-
gether undue control and direction over Irish educa-
tion. And some far-seeing people believe that the
ulterior object in view is that the Agricultural and
Technical or some similar department would, under the
coming scheme—which ““Inquirer ”’ and his friends are
endeavouring to smooth the way for—have charge of
our Primary and Intermediate systems, and that these
would be nominally managed (rather than controlled)
throughout the country by local committees much on
the lines that Technical Education is now. But such
2 consummation must be averted at any cost. Persons
who have had experience in the working of our existing
quasi-educational Committees, nominated by County
Councils and Corporations, know that whatever their
qualifications they are practically permitted little
liberty or initiative; they are cribbed, cabined, and
confined at every turn; and whenever recalcitrant in
carrying out the cut and dry schemes of the Depart-
ment, are brought to obedience by being docked or dis-
criminated against in the grant.

And the very Board of Technical Instruction itself,
with its twenty-one members, supposed to efficiently
represent the whole country, has, by all accounts, com-
paratively no more power than the least important
Committee in the country. The permanent officials,
somehow or other, manage to hoss the whole show;
and the state of things, if our Primary and Interme-
diate systems were administered on similar or cognate
principles, would logically be no better.

It would be much to be desired that persons who
have had no practical experionco in subjects of this
kind should refrain from laying down the law to thoso
who have served an apprenticeship to them, not to mind
telling them in effect that they are liars if they give in
good faith, for the information of your level-headed
readers, a recital of concrete facts of which they hiave
had personal knowledge.

But assuming that local Committees were in no way
dominated or hampered by a central body in the Metro-
polis, such as the Agricultural and Technical Depart-
ment of the present day, the question of their want of

qualification would still remain. T gave Starkie’s own
pessimistic opinion on this point in my last anticle, and
he went even further and stated explicitly that his own
experience of School Commiittees in Ireland did not
incline him to extend their operation.

And go anywhere in the provinces, into town or
rural -district, and propose to set up lay control over
the Primary Schools—Why, the people as a body
would laugh at the idea of your being able to secure
anything of a decent or effectivo personnel by ordinary
constitutional methods. Given, however, the_ wisdom
of Solomon, and the grace of God, and despotic power
of nomination, you might possibly succeed, but not
otherwise. The management of our I’rimrary_Edu('u-
tion, involving as it does in practice the religious in-
struction of the younger generation, and the safe-
guarding of their faith and morals, is on a very mngah
higher plane than the building of fences, the making
of roads and bridges, the appointment of dispensary
doctors, or the genera! run of the business of County
and District Councils. If “Inquirer’” cannot bear it in
mind, your intelligent readers, I am sure, will, that our
people as a body are only now emerging from the serf-
dom of centuries. Unaccustomed to responsibility,
they are as yet the merest tyros in the administration
of the most elementary forms of local government ; and
it will take a much longer apprenticeship in the
management of common business matters before our
Councils are fit to be entrusted—in the provinces at
all events—with even the partial control of education—
the highest rung in the ladder of Home Rule.

This so-called agitation for lay control is a parely
artificial and manufactured one. Not one in ten
thousand of the Catholic lay population take the
smallest interest in it. The people in general are in
fact ignorant of its very existence. I have myself
moved a good deal through the country and lived in
every province of it and in several of its principal
cities. I have mixed much with clergy and laity, and
have been intimately acquainted with a goodly number
of teachers—all highly classed men, too. I have heard
complaints, few and far between, against individual
clerical managers—whether well or ill-founded I can-
not say ; but in no quarter have I come across anything
in the nature of a proposal for local lay control. The
teachers themselves would spurn it, and the tax-payers
would not be burthened with the responsibility that it
would involve, even though it never brought a local
rate-in-aid in its train, as it is sure to do if established
in ouwr own day. The agitation is in fact engineered by
a few able and designing men in high places, who
find pliant propagandist tools in those young philoso-
phers, with their feet in the metropolitan mud and
their heads in the cosmopolitan ether, who go to
Russia for their sociology, to Germany for their econo-
mics and philosophy, to France for their dlerical views,
and to England for morbidly erotic and rotten fiction,
instead of setting up standards of their own, as our an-
cestors did when we were the light of Ewrope. What
a sad decadence, surely, for our scholars of to-day.
And in the matter of school management even “In-
quirer ”’ must, too, fall back on England for an
exemplar. Now the circumstances in both countries
are essentially different. In England the Catholics are
in almost an insignificant minority. Commencing with
voluntary schools, purely denominational, they have
naturally been doing their best to get, without alter-
ing this religious character, as much as they could of
any grants that were going, and particularly of that
from local taxation to which they directly contributed.
Unlike “ Inquirer,”’ T have not studied this question in
the “ Tablet,”” but I have had personal and practical
experience of it in many ways, and amongst these in
teaching the waifs and strays of my race in a night
school in a mining district in England.

Here in Ireland, with the Catholics vastly prepou-
derating, a ready-made system of Primary Education
was dumped down on us without asking even our leave.
Tt would be incorrect to call it secular, even in its
very inception : undenominational might be nearer the
mark. But whatever it was then, it is to all intents

~and purposes denominational now, and the very atmos-

phere of it over three-fourths of the country has been
so Catholic that, proportionately, far mo:-ey vocations
to the religious life come from the National Schools
than from “any other; and nobody, I believe, more
readily admits this than the Christian Brothers them-
selves, who are, so many of them, products of the sys-
tem. A more national spirit in the lesson books,
better provision for the native tongue, an increased
grant for the coustruction and maintenance of schools,
and to supplemont the inadequate salaries, and the
reformation of the National Board itself, are the things
that ave still to be desiderated. But tho money should
come from the Treasury, and not, as suggested in
Starkie’s Belfast address, and by masked wire-pullers
and conspirators in some of your contemporaries, from
& local rate, which, as stated in my last article, is
bound to be the concomitant of, or corollary to, local
lay control.

“ Inquirer’s *” childish perversion of logic in its appli-
cation to the performance of common council work by
clergymen, and his unwarranted assumption as to
“ Eireannac’’ and myself being in agreement with him
on certain points, T deem so unworthy of notice that I
shall not waste time or space in dealing with them.
His repudiation of the implied suggestion in his second
contribution in connection with his reference to the
non-remuneration of teachers for imparting religious
instruction, reminds me of the story of the man who
advised that somebody’s ears should not be nailed to
the pump. “Inquirer’’ has made as much capital of
the terms corruption, anti-clerical and anti-Catholic as
if my anticles had been salted with them, whereas I
never once used them. But if he would translate
favouritism begotten of nepotism, of consanguinity
and affimity, of trade and family and social influences,
into the term corruption, the responsibility is his, not
mine. ‘And, indeed, had he done so, he would be only
following the example of the enlightened and progres-
sive District Council referred to by him and me in
this correspondence, and who, since the present con-
troversy commenced, have actually, and in open meet-
ing, reported to the extent of a column and a half in
the press, accused themselves of corruption, without
putting a tooth on the term, and have, with hardly a
dissentient, put notice of a resolution on their agenda
acking that they should be dissolved and paid Guar-
dians sent to take their places.

And as to the assumption that an anti-clerical may
be a good Catholic, which a great apostle of this lay
control has sought to maintain in another quanter, I
shall only say that I have never known sound Catho-
licism to .prevail where anti-clericalism was in the as-
cendant. In this connection we are continually having
the abuses in the Church of France in pre-Revolution
days thrown in our faces; but these abuses had their
genesis in court intrigue and high politics, in that
Jansenism and Gallicanism, the doctrinal basis of which
has since been condemned by the Pope in Council as
formal heresy, and not in anything so democratic,
humble, and laudable as Primary School Management.

In taking my leave of “Inquirer,’”’ let me say that,
mere theorist and partisan as I took him to be, I never
hoped to change an iota of his pre-conceived views.
I have simply been giving my practical experience for
the information of your level-headed, fair-minded, and
enlightened readers, who on the evidence, supplemented
by their own personal knowledge, should have no diffi-
culty in judging between “Inquirer’’ and myself.
Cormac.

THE IRISH ATTITUDE TOWARDS FRANCE.

HE procession of St. Patrick’s Day has been the
means of bringing to light some curious, if not
nonsensical, opinions from both sides on the attitude we
ought to adopt towards the French Government. We
have heard, for instance, that a handful of Freemasons
and Jews has all but succeeded in uprooting the French
Church ; but we have also been told that Iris}l Catholics
have only one right—the right to say nothing. The
whole affair, both French and Irish, leads one to the
conclusion that the problem must be approached in an
eminently sane state of mind.

In the first place it is commonly argued that we
should preserve a discreet silence, principally for the
reason that we know nothing about the history and
political intrigues of the Catholic Church abroad! that
we only know one priest, the devoted Irish priest. In
other words, since we don’t fully understand the French
crisis, . we ought not to be making ourselves foolish-
looking under the eyes of the world. This is to philo-
sophise: nothing else. =~ Mr. R. Lowe, M.P., enter-
tained a profound detestation of views and opinions of
all sorts; but for this very reason he never succeeded
as a politician. His was a negative policy. He was
utterly impracticable. So is this. It is impossible as
well as undesirable, with the reign of the democracy of
mtellect in the offing, to suppress views and opinions,
however shallow they be. Let upholders of the oppo-
site theory apply their principle generally, as they are
bound to do, and what is the result? Why, the work-
man, so dear to democrats, must henceforth confine
himself to local affairs, because he knows only half the
truth about, say, Protection v. Free Trade. The Irish
people must not meddle with the University Question,
because the majority know next to nothing about higher
educational problems; nor, for that matter, with the
practical aspects of Home Rule, because they badly
understand the practical issues at stake. It is a blue
outlook for Democracy. The objection is worthy of
Mr. Balfour’s sophistry, and upsets all notions of rule
by the people. In that Republic we must not rest
satisfied that some should know everything about some-
thing, and others something about everything, but all
everything about everything. = No nation has ever
reached this stage of perfection, and probably never
will.

In the second place it is objected that the French
difficulty is of a political, and not at all of a religious
nature. This is an excessive demand on our intelli-
gence and common sense. Church Disestablishment, it
is true, is political and very little religious. But carried
through by Clemenceaus, and not by Gladstones, sus-
picions are aroused: Disestablishment becomes religious
and very litble political. But whether political or re-
ligious, it is the honourable custom of the Irish people
to extend its sympathy to oppressed peoples every-
where. The question then is—Ave French Catholics
being denied their liberty?  The minority in the
Chamber of Deputies say so.

It is felt in some quarters that this sympathy of ours
will give a shake to the growing friendship with the
Orange democrats. There ought to be no danger for
that delicate bud. The Catholicity—or rather Chris-
tianity—of Dublin is robust, but not aggressive. The
French Government are not marching Protestantwards.
Neither has the campaign the negative object of secu-
lanising public life: it is positive, to leave Christians as
rave on the banks of the Seine as the red Indian on the
shores of the Manhattan.

Now a deal of deliberate misunderstanding is indulged
in as to the views of Irish Catholics. It is decidedly
unfair to base an attack on the assumption that we
regrot the Disestablishment of the French Church. I
find myself unable to quote a single Irish Catholic
authority, lay or cleric, in favour of perpetuating the
State Church. What Catholics object to is this—when
the French Government freed itself of the Church that
it did not also free the Church of the Government.

Trish Catholics have learned the value of a Church
supported by the people. We would not tolerate any

other. We are able to make a fair guess at what a
State-endowed Catholic Church in Ireland would be

ke.  Therefore, in translating the St. Patrick’s Day
Procession into a protest against the Disestablishment of
the French Church some of our weckly and monthly
Teviews are entirely out of order.

It is also maintained that Irench Churchmen, by
refusing to agreo to the formation of the Committees of
Public Worship, have themselves to blame for the con-
sequences. This is the only debatable point raised by
Inish Catholic democrats. From the political point of
view (but from no other) it might be argued that the
Bishops were ill-advised in refusing to sanction the for-
mation of these Committees. We are liable to forget,
however, that political expedioncy in religious matfers
is not at one with Catholic standards and ideals. . It
is just the point where Catholicists are given to disagree
with politicians. As in Germauny the Catholics wished
to support the Socialists, but the German Bishops
objected ; so in France the Bishops and French Catho-
lics readily divined the benign care under which they
were about to be placed.  But more than this, they
foresaw that the Iierarchal principle of the Churel
would be extinguished, and the essen digaity
Catholicity go unrecognised. 1t is politi traizing
cnly that would teach Catholics to allow Churel and
Cathedrals to be placed on the same legal footing as
Parisian music-halls, and subject to the same interfer-
ences.  Ought the ‘“ Associations Cultuelles ” to have
been accepted? He who understauds Catholicism has
no two opinions.

as a class; and adds that he does not mean ‘‘ the mys-
tical hero, whose cloudy home is the Abbey Theatre,””
nor ‘‘the amateur hero, the life-saver of our times,”
but what he calls the professional hero, the protagonist
of romance. His answer is that they are an extinct
| class; that most unfit, they have failed to survive in
modern life.

Had they then an existence? We have only to read
an epic poem, a medieval chronicle, a chapter of his-
s tory to answer that they had. Arms and the man are
the theme of the great epics of the world. The armed
hero rides forth in quest of adventure in the medieval
romances; he looms large in Elizabethan England, in
contemporary France, in Spain, in Portugal, Italy,
Germany and the northern kingdoms. He re-appears
in the Thirty Years’ War; is with the captains of the
18th century, and follows Napoleon across Iurope.
Alive in the beginning of the 19th century, has he
survived into the 20th? 1 think we may answer, he
has—for the type is eternal, only the conditions change.

The type may cover, and does, of course, men of
most diverse characters. But they are alike in these
things, they possess great animal courage, and have a
love of adventure. Animal courage and the desire for
action where physical danger is encountered, with vary-
ing change of sceme, are the essentials of the adven-
ture-hero. . Given this basis to start from, he may
differ in character as much from another of his genus
as Napoleon and his marshals do from a mystic like
 General Gordon. The writer of an adventure-story
~ dealing with war cannot ignore him ; the type is there;
~ he meets it in every history, every chronicle read in.
preparation of the work.  So he ta.kes'it, _Iets his crea:tlvc
faculty clothe the figure, and, if an indifferent writer,
may do it badly, but he has seen a real personality.
He can choose the mercenary whose aim is pay and
plunder, fighting on .this side, now on bhu.‘b,‘ whom he
finds earning his wage in the pages of Troissart, plun-
déring behind Gustavus Adolphus, Tilly and Wallen-.

d living at’free quarbers as he marches in
len” armies.  Scott took him, and his

THE. LEADER.

May 11, 1907.

Or he may take what appears the more conventional
character of the type, the brave man, ambitious,
honourable, who enlists in ‘a foreign army, and gives
faithful service to the master he has chosen. This is
a world-wide specimen of the adventure-hero, and every
age has produced him. o :

Or if the scene of the story be some striking episode
in war, some moment when a people make a final stand
for liberty, he can take for his adventure-hero the man
trained to arms who fights for his country. In such a
scene as the Siege of Limerick there were men whom
the history of the time had rendered pcpmless who
might have recovered their fortunes by going over to
the other side, but who clung to the losing one because
it was the national one. Men of this class belong to
the highest order of the type, and are no more extinct
to-day than the life-saver who, when he springs into
deep water to save a human being from drowning, or
otherwise risks his own life, is animated by the same
courage as the man who fights to save his country.

Of course the writer- may fail to make the figure
alive to his readers. That is his own fault; the failure
the result of his own limitations. But therc is this to
be said—that the adventure-hero lends himself more
casily to over-colour, or a wooden presentment, than
another type of hero. It is light work to smother him
with the incidents, or leave him with no more substance
than a shadow, while plot and accident, love and war,
move round him. Or 1t is easy to put too much blood
in his veins, to make him purple, thunderous, the hero
of melodrama. In the construction of an adventure-
story dealing with war, the writer has to consider, if he
wishes to do his work well or passing well, the essentials
of such a story. The movement must be rapid, con-
nected, striking if he can make it, incident being more
important than character. Yet character, too, has to
be regarded, though adventure weighs down the scale.
ITe has to consider the time he has chosen, the spirit
of the period, the view of life taken then by men of
war, the callousness or passions of the soldiers, the en-
vironment which he must reconstruct.  All this he
must do, and then create his story. There have to be
scenes of peril, danger to the hero, a successful ending.
Such things are the essence of the adventure-tale. In-
trospection, dissection of character, analysis of emo-
tions are out of place in a story of action. ‘In Sars-
field’s Days,”” the story that led  Chanel ”’ to ask if
the hero of romance was extinct as a class, the scene is
laid at the end of the 17th century, a century that had
seen the Thirty Years’ War, the civil wars of France and
those of England, the wars of Louis XIV.; and which
went out in a false peace, men stiff standing to their
arms.  Such a century bred the type in abuudance.
Choosing such a period, taking a heroic moment in a
nation’s history, Iveagh (the character declared extinct
by ““Chanel ”’) had to be the product of his century.
Moreover, as the hero of an adventure-story he had
to do very much what I made him do. But ‘* Chanel *’
is not quite just when he says he made a prodigious ass
of himself on the smallest available opportunity. Iveagh
went where he thought honour led lim ; placed himself
in danger to help Sarsfield’s wife; played cards like
every other soldier in all the camps of Europe; fell in
love with a girl with whom not only his friend but
half the Irish officers were in love ; and had very gcod
reasons for delaying the execution of Manus O’Brien.

As to the heroine ‘“ Chanel *’ will have none of her,
and declaves she has a very unlovely character. But
I am writing of the adventure-hero, not of heroines,

So I leave Ethne, to whom I tried to give some indivi 2

duality, to the judgment of those who read the story.

3 ¢ ¥ NQUIRER *’ poses as an honest seeker of truth
I who would promote rather than destroy phe in-

y " terests of Catholicity in Ireland. He is anxious' to
know what ¢ ecclesiastical law ’’ requires in the matter
of managing primary schools, but, as far as I have been
able to observe, he has not defined what he means by
‘the rather vague term, ‘‘ecclesiastical law.”” ~ While
waiting for his definition we may go on with the facts—
(1) that the present system is now a long time in pos-
session, and (2) that those in this country who 9],.1’0111(1
best know the requirements of “‘ ecclesiastical law *’ are
strongly opposed to many of the suggested changes.
" The present system has now held the field for more
than seventy years; it has helped to preserve, in face
many difficulties, the comparatively healthy Catholic
t that prevails in Ireland ; and to & somewhat simi-
stem of parochial school management in Australia

THE LEADER.

May 11, 1907.

and the United States are due the vigorous progress and
hopeful outlook of the Church in those lands. —The
so-called ‘ Catholic systems’’ which evoke the admira-
tion of the ‘‘educated layman’’ have not achieved in
Turopean countries—France among t].)e numbe.r—the
happy results one might reasonably desire; and if they
have any lessons for us in Ireland, it would be to avoid
taking them for our models, and to keep grimly and
tenaciously the firm grip we now have of our holdings
in the National Schools.

T must not be taken to imply that laymen are to have
no effective control or share in directing the system of
secular education in Ireland. No sane man would
maintain such a position. Have they not always had a
dominant influence in this matter? Catholic ecclesias-
tics have. never been numerously represented on the
National Rducation Board. For the greater part of
the Board’s existence they had no representation at all.
To ‘this Board we have always owed the programmes
prescribed in secular subjects; it is they who decided
the literary qualifications required in the teachers; they
appointed the inspectors whose duty it was to see that
their rules were observed and secular subjects sufficiently
taught, and with them rested the power to withdraw
all public aid from any school if the teacher appointed
by the local manager failed to give satisfaction. And
yet it is insinuated that laymen have not had an
adequate share in directing secular education! To be
sure, they have not the right to decide who the indivi-
dual teacher is to be; it would be an evil day if they
had. But it would be an equally serious misfortune to
have the teacher selected by a majority vote of the local
Council, many of whose members may have no interest
in the pupils or the school. To the teachers are en-
trusted the most sacred interests of the nation, and it
would be a public calamity to have them selected for
their responsible positions by a system of voting which
would give the most stupid and vicious and corrupt of
the electors a power equal to that possessed by the most
conscientious and enlightened member of the community.
The great bulk of sensible Catholic parents will not

expose the temporal and eternal interests of their chil- .

dren to such hazard.

““ Inquirer ’’ appears to attach much importance to
the share of ‘‘lay control’’ admitted in the Catholic
Primary Schools of England. Does he not know that
such control was forced on the schools by the regulations

of a Protestant Education Department in Protestant -

England, while its natural evil effects have been avoided
through the circumstance that the practical selection of
the Catholic lay managers always remained in the hands
of the clerical manager or rector of the mission?

““ Inquirer ”’ calls teachers the ¢‘servants’’ of the
National Board. Who are the Commissioners of
National Education that the teachers should be called
their ‘“ servants?’’ The teachers are the servants of the

pupils and of the pupils’ parents by whose money they *
are paid. The Commissioners have as much claim to-

the money as a bank clerk has to the gold which he
hands to the customer outside the counter. The title
““ civil authority * is, I dare say, meant to be imposing.
Tt can be so only within its own limited sphere, and it
outsteps its sphere when it would ask the parent to
resign his right to select a teacher for his own children.
The ordinary Catholic parent has much more confi-
dence in his priest than in the local Council or in the
Board of National Education.

As to the managers being needlessly haunted by the

memory of Whately, it will be enough to say that the

present time has dangers as insidious and more ruinous
than those which threatened in the days of Whately,
and among the most powerful ‘of these evil agencies is
the commanding influence in the formation of public
opinion possessed by certain well-known Catholic oppo-
nents of clerical management of Primary Schools.
